meta.title: Demonstrate understanding of significant aspects of unfamiliar texts
meta.purpose: Students are able to demonstrate understanding of significant aspects of unfamiliar texts.

grade_descriptors.N1: Evidence does not reach the minimum threshold for Achieved-level indicators.
grade_descriptors.N2: Some Achieved-level indicators are met, but not enough to satisfy all required Achieved criteria.
grade_descriptors.A3: All Achieved-level indicators are met; no Merit-level indicator meets the Merit threshold.
grade_descriptors.A4: All Achieved indicators are met and at least one Merit indicator is met, but overall performance is below the Merit threshold.
grade_descriptors.M5: All Achieved indicators are met and the Merit threshold is reached; no Excellence-level indicator meets the Excellence threshold.
grade_descriptors.M6: All Achieved indicators are met, all Merit indicators are met, and at least one Excellence indicator is met, but overall performance is below the Excellence threshold.
grade_descriptors.E7: All Achieved and Merit indicators are met and the Excellence threshold is reached, but not all Excellence indicators are met.
grade_descriptors.E8: All Achieved, Merit, and Excellence indicators are met.

key_terms.definition: Selected from purposes and audiences; ideas; language features; structures; text conventions.
Examples of specific and relevant details from the text(s) used to support ideas.

omis.description: Describes how meanings and effects are created in significant aspects of texts and points to those details.

positive_signals: Explicit phrases describing how features create meaning or effect, e.g., 'creates meaning by...', 'achieves effect through...'
Pointing to specific details or quotes from the text
Clear explanation of the feature's impact on meaning or effect
Direct linkage between the aspect and its contribution
negative_signals: Naming a feature without stating its effect
Mentioning a device without explaining its contribution
General statements lacking specific textual support
evidence_guide.common_confusion: Students often name literary devices but fail to explain their specific role in creating meaning or effect.

success_examples: describing how meanings and effects are created

omis.description: Selects and uses specific and relevant details from the text(s) as supporting evidence for ideas.

positive_signals: Use of specific quotes or details from the text
Direct relevance of evidence to the stated idea
Avoidance of general plot summary
Clear linkage of evidence to support the idea
negative_signals: General plot recall without specific details
Unrelated quotations or examples
Vague or irrelevant evidence
evidence_guide.common_confusion: Students often provide plot summaries instead of specific, relevant details that directly support their ideas.

success_examples: examples of specific and relevant details

omis.description: Identifies aspects related to purposes and audiences in the text and links them to meaning or effect.

positive_signals: Explicit reference to purpose or audience
Connection of purpose/audience to a textual aspect
Linkage to meaning or effect
Avoidance of mere naming without connection
negative_signals: Merely naming an audience without linking to aspect
Stating purpose without connecting to meaning/effect
Isolated mentions without integration
evidence_guide.common_confusion: Students often name the audience or purpose but fail to explain how it relates to the text's meaning or effect.

omis.source_ref: AS PDF p2 Explanatory Notes 2 (list of significant aspects)

omis.description: Identifies and discusses ideas such as character, theme, or setting as significant aspects contributing to meaning.

positive_signals: Identification of specific ideas like character, theme, setting
Discussion linking the idea to meaning or effect
Avoidance of mere listing without linkage
Explanation of contribution to overall meaning
negative_signals: Listing an idea without linking to meaning
Stating theme/character without discussion
Isolated identification without effect
evidence_guide.common_confusion: Students often identify ideas but fail to discuss how they contribute to the text's meaning.

omis.description: Identifies language features (e.g., figurative language, style, syntax, symbolism, vocabulary) and states their effect.

positive_signals: Naming specific language features like metaphor, syntax
Stating the effect created, e.g., mood, emphasis
Clear linkage between feature and its impact
Avoidance of mere naming without effect
negative_signals: Naming a feature without stating its effect
Listing features without explanation of impact
Generic mentions without specific effects
evidence_guide.common_confusion: Students often name language features but forget to explain the effect they create on the reader or text.

success_examples: language features (such as figurative language, style, syntax, symbolism, vocabulary)
omis.source_ref: AS PDF p2 Explanatory Notes 2 (language features)

omis.description: Recognises structures (part/whole, narrative) or text conventions (spelling, punctuation, grammar) as significant aspects shaping meaning.

positive_signals: Identification of structures like narrative, part/whole
Recognition of conventions like punctuation, grammar
Connection to how they shape meaning
Avoidance of mere noting without linkage
negative_signals: Merely noting a paragraph or punctuation without connection
Listing structures/conventions without meaning link
Isolated mentions without effect on meaning
evidence_guide.common_confusion: Students often point out structures or conventions but fail to explain how they contribute to shaping the text's meaning.

success_examples: structures (such as part text, whole text, narrative)
text conventions (such as spelling, punctuation, grammar)
omis.source_ref: AS PDF p2 Explanatory Notes 2 (structures; text conventions)

omis.description: Explains how significant aspects of texts work together to create meaning and effects, using supporting evidence.

positive_signals: Use of linkage words like 'together', 'combine', 'reinforce'
Connection of at least two aspects to a shared effect
Explanation of combined impact on meaning/effects
Avoidance of separate listings without integration
negative_signals: Listing features separately without linkage
Describing aspects in isolation
Lack of words showing combination or reinforcement
evidence_guide.common_confusion: Students often describe individual aspects but fail to explain how they work together to create overall meaning and effects.

success_examples: explaining how significant aspects of texts work together

omis.description: Explains how choices (e.g., mood/tone, ideas, features) impact reader understanding or response, supported by quotations.

positive_signals: Cause-effect link between choice and reader impact
Explanation of how mood/tone/ideas/features affect understanding/response
Support with quotations
Avoidance of generic statements without specifics
negative_signals: Generic statements of effect without cause
Lack of connection to reader understanding/response
Absence of supporting quotations
evidence_guide.common_confusion: Students often describe effects but fail to link them clearly to specific textual choices and their impact on readers.

omis.source_ref: Exam Booklet p2-6 question stems and instruction lines; 91927-exm-2024

omis.description: Integrates ideas, language features, and structure within a coherent explanation of meaning/effect.

positive_signals: Weaving of multiple aspects (ideas, features, structure) into one argument
Coherent explanation connecting aspects
Avoidance of separate paragraphs without linkage
Integrated discussion of meaning/effect
negative_signals: Separate paragraphs that never connect aspects
Isolated discussions of individual aspects
Lack of integration into a single argument
evidence_guide.common_confusion: Students often discuss aspects separately but fail to integrate them into a cohesive explanation of meaning or effect.

omis.source_ref: AS PDF p1-2 Achievement/Merit scope and aspect list

omis.description: Discusses the relationship between significant aspects and the writer’s purpose, using supporting evidence.

positive_signals: Clear statement of writer's purpose
Linkage of purpose to identified aspects
Use of supporting evidence
Avoidance of naming purpose without connection
negative_signals: Naming purpose without linking to aspects
Lack of evidence supporting the relationship
Isolated statements without discussion
evidence_guide.common_confusion: Students often state the writer's purpose but fail to discuss its relationship to specific textual aspects with evidence.

success_examples: discussing the relationship between significant aspects of texts

omis.description: Discusses how significant aspects relate to wider context (e.g., cultural, social), supported by specific textual evidence.

positive_signals: Explicit naming of wider context (cultural, social)
Explanation of relationship to aspects
Support with specific textual evidence
Avoidance of vague mentions of 'context'
negative_signals: Vague mentions of 'context' without specifics
Lack of explanation of relationship
Absence of textual evidence
evidence_guide.common_confusion: Students often mention 'context' vaguely but fail to explicitly name and relate it to textual aspects with evidence.

omis.description: When a text is based in a Māori context, acknowledges this context and relates it to meaning or purpose with evidence.

positive_signals: Explicit acknowledgment of Māori context
Connection of context to meaning or purpose
Use of evidence to support the relation
Applies only when text is in Māori context
negative_signals: Failure to connect context to meaning/purpose
Lack of evidence
Ignoring Māori context when applicable
evidence_guide.common_confusion: Students may acknowledge the context but fail to explicitly relate it to the text's meaning or purpose with evidence.

success_examples: One of the unfamiliar texts will be based in a Māori context.
